title: Market segmentation
summary: 9609 AS — segmentation bases, targeting, positioning, and applying segments to the marketing mix.
learningObjectives: Evaluate benefits and limitations of segmentation.
Apply segmentation to case study marketing plans.

simpleExplanation.title: Divide to conquer
simpleExplanation.summary: Segmentation splits a market into groups with similar needs so the firm can target the most attractive segments with a tailored marketing mix.
steps: Segment using demographic, geographic, psychographic, behavioural bases.
Position the brand clearly in customers' minds.

flashcards.back: Dividing a market into distinct groups with similar characteristics or needs.
Age, gender, income, occupation, family size.
Usage rate, loyalty, benefits sought, occasion.
Choosing which segment(s) to focus marketing resources on.
How customers perceive the product vs competitors — unique space in the mind.

sections.content: **Market segmentation** (3.1.6) is central to AS marketing. Learn **STP**: Segment → Target → Position. Every **mix recommendation** should name the **target segment** first.

items: **Demographic** — easy to measure; age/income common in exams.

sections.content:
**Targeting** — evaluate segment size, growth, competition, fit with firm strengths.

**Positioning** — perceptual map: e.g. "affordable luxury", "greenest option". Must be **delivered** consistently through the 4Ps.

sections.question: A gym chain considers segments: (A) students 18–24, (B) professionals 30–45, (C) retirees 60+. Recommend one target segment and outline positioning.
sections.solution:
**Target segment B — professionals 30–45.**

**Why:** Higher **income** for membership and personal training; **time-poor** — value convenience (early/late hours); large segment in urban locations; willing to pay for **health and status** benefits.

**Positioning:** "Premium efficient fitness for busy careers" — **product:** express classes, app booking; **price:** mid-high; **promotion:** LinkedIn, corporate partnerships; **place:** city centres near offices.

Students (A) may be **price-sensitive**; retirees (C) may need **special facilities** — viable as secondary segments later.

sections.content: Use a **perceptual map** sketch in evaluate questions — two axes (e.g. price vs quality) with brands plotted.
